ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The NCQD clusters under a transmission electron microscope (TEM), (B) the size distribution, (C) and elemental analysis using energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectroscopy; (D) fluorescent stereomicroscopy (330 – 380 nm), color of NCQDs under (E) visible and (F) UV light. (G) absorbance (NCQD0.02 solution) and (H) emission spectra (UV excitation of 320 – 390 nm); (I) the X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectroscopy; (J) zeta potential of as-prepared NCQDs.

(A) XPS survey, N1s, O1s, C1s spectra, (B) Transmittance (%) spectra by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy; (C) NMR (13C and 1H) spectra in DMSO-d6 (solvent); (D) viability(%) of HeLa cell lines after NCQD exposure of 48 h.

Seed priming with NCQDs significantly (p<0.01) improved seed germination (A), root growth (B), root dry weight (C), shoot length (D) and shoot dry weight (E) root in maize; and chlorophyl a (F), chlorophyl b (G) and carotenoid (H) contents as compared to control (c) treatment. The errors bars correspond to standard deviation (n=6).

Impacts of NCQDs (20 μg/mL) supplied on D0, D7, D14 and D21 i.e. 7th, 14th, 21st and 28th day of germination on maize plants in hydroponics in terms of maximum root length (A), plant height (B), fresh shoot (C), fresh root weights (D), dry shoot (E) and dry root (F) weights. The errors bars correspond to standard deviation (n=5, p<0.01).

Comparisons of ETR (A), TR (B) and ФPSII (C) of maize plants applied with NCQD and control (without NCQDs) in response to different Qamb values (100 – 400 µmol.m−2.s−1). Maize plants treated with NCQD0.02 (D), and control (E) for visual comparison. Relative expression of genes for (F) phosphoenolpyruvate carboxylase (ZmPEPC), (G) ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate carboxylase/ oxygenase (ZmRUB), (H) pyruvate orthophosphate dikinase (ZmPPDK), (I) NADP-malic enzyme (ZmNADP-ME), (J) NADP-malate dehydrogenase (ZmNADP-MDH) and (K) carbonic anhydrase (ZmCA) in treated plants as compared to control.

Localization of fluorescent NCQDs observed within guard cells (GC=white arrows) in treated plant (A) and control (B); in intercellular spaces (ICS=yellow arrows) in treated plants (C) and control plants (D); within cytoplasm (Cyt) in treated (E) and control (f), indicating cellular localization (green arrows).
A pictorial illustration of proposed chemical synthesis of fluorescent NCQDs derived from proteins and starch molecules in whole-grain wheat flour.
A schematic illustration showing the translocation of NCQDs from the roots into various leaf tissues.

The illustrative contribution of bioderived NCQDs in boosting photosynthesis, stomatal conductance, CO2 fixation, electron transport chain and upregulating associated genes in maize chloroplast, leading to higher starch synthesis.

head.ce:title: Bioderived carbon quantum dots boost maize growth and photosynthesis by augmenting UV spectrum absorption and carbon assimilation regulatory genes

ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of green and eco-friendly nanomaterials generated from diverse organic carbon sources (bioderived) has been the subject of substantial research in recent years [1–4]. A procedure known as "green synthesis" involves synthesizing materials with less harmful chemicals and energy-intensive conditions by utilizing sustainable and ecologically safe approaches [5]. Carbon dots (CDs), a novel class of carbon-based nanomaterials, have gained attention due to their important applications in a variety of domains in medical sciences [3,4,6–13]. Because of their fluorescence emission, Sun et al. (2006) referred to these carbon nanoparticles as "carbon quantum dots (CQDs)" [14–18]. Similar to other carbonaceous materials, CQDs are less hazardous as compared to other metal-based nanomaterials. However, metal-based CQDs may also exhibit toxicity at larger concentrations [19]. Modifying their surface with various functional groups, also known as surface functionalization, has been adopted by various researchers to reduce the cytotoxic effects of CQDs and improve their biocompatibility [20]. Therefore, utilizing bioderived functionalized CQDs has a great potential as a secure way to use CQDs in agricultural contexts.

The Food and Agriculture Organization (FAO) and the World Bank reported that around 20–30 % of stored grain is wasted every year [21,22]. Approximately 9 % of the stored wheat grains is wasted in developed countries, whereas in developing countries, owing to the lack of appropriate storage facilities, this percentage may increase to 20 % or more [23]. Pest infestation often leads to undesirable odors, alterations in taste, and contamination, which in turn cause customer aversion and a significant reduction in prices [24]. Wheat flour contains significant amounts of carbon (C) and nitrogen (N), in the form of different starch (70 – 75 %) and proteins (8 – 11 %), and both C and N are essential elements for plant growth [25,26]. N is essential for the biosynthesis of proteins, nucleic acids, and other cellular components (chlorophyl); whereas C is a fundamental component of photosynthesis, starch biosynthesis, and other structural components (cellulose). Hence, depreciated and devalued stored grains that include these constituents may be used as bio-derived nanomaterials. Such organic or bioderived nanomaterials may serve as eco-friendly and sustainable alternatives for utilizing wasted and discarded grains [27]. Therefore, using green synthesized and organic material based CQDs to increase biomass and photosynthetic efficiency in plants is a safer and more environmentally friendly strategy to improve crop yield.

Fluorescent CQDs can be used in plants, in order to maximize solar absorption and its transformation into photosynthetically active radiations (λ = 400–700 nm) for photosynthesis, by adjusting their absorbance and fluorescence [3,28–33]. Prior preliminary research has indicated that vascular plants containing both chlorophyll a and chlorophyll b exhibit good photosynthetic efficiency with respect to the blue (400–500 nm) and red (650–680 nm) absorption peaks, respectively [34–36]. As a result, far-blue and violet light (<400 nm) contribute very little to photosynthesis [37]. Therefore, optimizing the CQDs to convert the portion of sunlight that isn't suitable for photosynthesis into radiation that the photosynthetic apparatus can use (PAR) is a very efficient way to get the most of solar energy. Heavy metals are known to accumulate in the environment as a result of the use of chemical fertilizers to increase crop yield, which is very harmful [38–42]. Comparably, it has also been observed that metal-based nanomaterials have the capacity to exhibit toxicity at high doses [43,44]. Some metal-based CQDs have been shown in numerous studies to have a detrimental effect on cell membranes, leading to oxidative stress and inflammatory reactions in living things [45–48]. Adopting creative and effective solutions that are both economically feasible and environmentally benign is crucial to increasing agricultural output and meeting the demands of a world population that is growing at a rapid rate [38,49,50].

In certain investigations, carbon sources and nitrogen-containing precursors were employed together during the synthesis process to enable the formation of carbon dots with nitrogen atoms embedded in their structure [51–55], which changed the surfaces of the CQDs [56–58]. The addition of nitrogen can change the photoluminescence properties of CQDs, causing them to emit light in different colors [59], alter their electrical characteristics [60], and add more energy levels to the carbon dot structure, which improves charge transfer [61]. Functional groups containing nitrogen also improve the dispersibility, stability and biocompatibility [62–64] of CQDs in aqueous and organic solutions. These properties can facilitate improved interaction with biological systems in plants. Consequently, the goal of the research was to ascertain the distribution of N-functionalized organic CQDs inside plant tissues in order to enhance photosynthesis and growth, as well as how they interact with various photosynthesis related genes. Furthermore, the study offers a sustainable and efficient approach to utilizing the florescent properties of bioderived NCQDs by absorbing the UV spectrum of sunlight and emitting blue portion of PAR, mimicking chlorophyll a and b, in order to enhance the photosynthetic activity and growth in maize plants.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Green synthesis of nitrogen functionalized CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Whole-grain wheat flour (WF) was utilized as the carbon precursor for green synthesis of N-functionalized CQDs using a novel two-step synthesis; firstly, hydrolyzing starch and protein molecules using 0.25 M hydrochloric acid (HCl); and in second step, microwave-assisted rearrangement [65,66] of fragments (hydrolyzed molecules) under heat produced by oscillation due to microwave radiations. One gram (1 g) of whole-grain wheat flour was combined with 5 mL of ultra-pure water. Subsequently, 1 milliliter of 0.25 M HCl was added gradually (dropwise) while constantly swirling (500 rpm) the mixture with magnetic stirrer and the mixture was continuously agitated at 40 ºC for next 24 hours. A preliminary study was conducted to optimize the minimum required temperature and reaction time in microwave heating. After ultracentrifuging (10,000 rpm), the supernatant containing HCl was disposed of and the pellet was cleaned using ultrapure water (5 mL). The operation was executed twice by adding ultra-pure water in order to fully eliminate any remaining traces of HCl from the hydrolyzed material. The mixture was then microwaved for 1.5 minutes at 800 W power level and the light-yellow paste was then cooled down by adding ultra-pure water (5 mL) dropwise. Subsequently, the as-prepared viscous solution containing fragments was centrifuged at 100 rpm for ten minutes. The rotational speed was increased from 100 to 500 rpm in 10-minute cycles each. The supernatant containing the nanosized NCQD solution was collected, and a 0.22 µm filter was employed to exclude contaminants from the liquid component. For further purification, the filtrate was undergone dialysis against Milli-Q water (MW, Millipore, Gradient A-10, Burlington, MA) for two days under continuous stirring. From this highly pure NCQD solution, ivory-white colored powder of N-functionalized carbon quantum dots was produced by freeze-drying (FR Drying Dig Unit, Model 230, USA). The average conversion yield of as-synthesized NCQDs was recorded to be 8.3 %. The obtained powder was thereafter stored in a cool (20 – 25 ºC), dry, and light-free container for future use.

The samples were analyzed using a 120 kV acceleration voltage transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEM-1230, JEOL, Akishima, Japan) using a small amount of the as-prepared NCQDs, previously disseminated in ethanol. The UV–vis spectra for absorbance (250 – 700 nm) and florescence (400 – 700 nm) under different excitation wavelengths (310 – 400 nm) were measured with an infinite 200Pro spectrophotometer (MicroPlate Reader, TECAN, Switzerland). Size distribution and surface charge of as-prepared NCQDs were measured using a zeta potential analyzer (Zetasizer Nano ZS90, Malvern, UK). The samples were diluted in water to a concentration of 1 mg/mL and then subjected to analysis. The analysis was performed in triplicates, with each run consisting of 20 repetitions, and the mean value of the triplicates was used. A spectrometer (Thermo Nicolet 6700) was used to acquire the Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra, which span a wavelength range of 4000–650 cm−1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was studied using a spectrophotometer (Axis Supra+, Japan). An inverted fluorescent microscope (Nikon DS-Ri2) was used to analyze the cells using fluorescence imaging. Two-photon laser pulses, with a wavelength of 330–380 nm, were used to excite the CQDs. For elemental analysis of as-prepared NCQDs, energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) (FEI Quanta, model FEG 650, US), and X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Bruker D8 Advance, US), were employed. Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectra for proton (1H) and carbon (13C) were measured by dissolving as-prepared NCQD (10 mg) in dimethyl sulfoxide-d6 (DMSO-d6) as a solvent (0.6 mL) and recorded on an NMR spectrometer (Bruker Avance 400 MHz).

Cytotoxicity or cell viability testing was performed using the 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) cell cytotoxicity assay kit (Beyotime, China; Cat No. C0009S) according to the manufacturer's instructions [67,68]. HeLa cells were seeded in a 96-well cell culture plate (5000 cells per well) and incubated in fresh cell culture medium (Wuhan Sunncell Biotech Co., Ltd., Cat No. SNLM-013) containing 10 % fetal bovine sera (FBS) and 1 % P/S [69] at 37 °C in a 5.0 % CO2 environment for 12 h. NCQD stock solutions (0.02, 0.2, 2, 20, 200 and 2000 µg/mL) were prepared at a concentration of 0.02, 0.2, 2, 20, 200 and 2000 µg/mL in phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) and filtered (0.22 μm). The cells were treated with prepared NCQD solutions for 48 h. The MTT solution (5 mg/mL) was added to each well (20 μL per well) and incubated for another 3–4 h. Hereafter, the supernatant was discarded, and purple insoluble MTT product (formazan crystals) was redissolved in DMSO (200 μL) for 6 h. The MTT product concentration in each well was detected using a spectrophotometer adjusted at OD570.

The seeds (maize crop) were initially rinsed with MilliQ water (MW, Millipore, Gradient A-10, Burlington, MA) and surface sterilized for fifteen minutes with a 10 % sodium hypochlorite solution. Subsequently, the seeds were again rinsed three times with MilliQ water. Three different treatments were identified by different NCQD gradients (NCQD0.02, NCQD0.04, and NCQD0.06) corresponding to NCQD concentrations of 0.02, 0.04, and 0.06 mg/g of seed, respectively. The NCQD solutions were ultrasonically dispersed for 15 minutes to provide the best possible dispersion. Furthermore, the experimental setting contained a control treatment (designated as C) that did not include NCQDs. Before the experiment, each seed lot was given a single rinse with respective doses of NCQD for twelve hours. Seed germination sheets (10 cm × 15 cm) were stacked and placed into identically sized plastic petri dishes. There were ten seeds in each petri dish, and each treatment had six replications. The incubation chamber was kept at 25 ± 2 °C with a 95 % relative humidity level as the experimental conditions. The data measurements of seed germination (%) were taken at the end of the second day. Following their transfer, the germinated seeds were placed in plastic pots fitted with foam sponges to provide support the plants, and the pots were then filled with double-distilled water containing nutritional solution (Hoagland's media) [70]. The plants were grown under regulated environmental conditions in a greenhouse, including temperature regimes of 28–30°C, a photoperiod of 10–14 hours (600 mol m−2 s−1 intensity), and 70 % humidity. Following the sixth day, measurements of the seedling parameters, such as root-shoot fresh weights, dry weights, and maximum lengths, were collected.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Root application of NCQDs in hydroponics
ce:section.ce:para: A study using hydroponics was carried out to explore the effects of NCQDs on maize growth physiology. After three days of germination, the equally growing seedlings were transferred to 30 ×15 x 15 cm sized sterile boxes. Each of these boxes held 54 seedlings (5 rows and 9 plants per row), and they were fitted with foam sponges to hold the plants. Following seedling transfer, the boxes were filled with double-distilled water containing full-strength nutrient solution (Hoagland's media) [70]. The boxes were kept at an optimum temperature of 28±2 ◦C inside a controlled growth room with a photoperiod of 10–14 hours (600 μmol m−2 s−1 intensity), and 70 % humidity. The study comprised of two treatment groups: one experimental group received a treatment dose of NCQD0.02 (20 µg/mL or 0.02 g/L) and the other (control) group received no NCQDs. Every 84 hours, the seedlings in both treatment groups received their nutritive media solutions; whereas the treatment group received NCQD (0.02 g/L) treatment on the 7th (D0), 14th (D7), 21st (D14) and 28th (D21) days after germination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photosynthesis and biomass related trait measurements
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to assess the effects of NCQDs on growth physiology, a number of parameters related to maximum root length, plant height, fresh root-shoot weights, and dried root-shoot weights were measured on the 7th, 14th, 21st, 28th, and 35th day following seed germination, denoted as D0, D7, D14, D21 and D28 respectively. NCQD solution was applied to plants on 7th (D0) and 21st (D14) day after germination in the treatment group. On the other hand, 28 days (D21) following seed germination information regarding traits related to photosynthesis, such as intercellular CO2 concentration (Ci), transpiration rate (E), photosynthetic efficiency (ФP) and stomatal conductance (Gs) were measured using an infrared gas analyzer (Li-Cor 6400XT, Lincoln, NE, USA), whereas chlorophyl a (chl a), chlorophyl b (chl b), carotenoid contents from fresh leaves, were measured using an UV spectrophotometer (BioTek® 05404–0998, USA) at wavelengths of 470, 646.8 and 663.2 nm, respectively, following the formula given by Lichtenthaler and Buschmann (2001) [71]. The lengths from the collar region to the growing tip of the shoot and primary root, respectively, were used to measure the lengths of the shoot and roots, respectively. A thread was used to straighten any bent shoots or roots, and the measured values were expressed in centimeters. The root lengths were measured using java-based ImageJ program (version 1.54i). In order to evaluate dry matter, the seedlings were weighed both before and after being dried in a hot air oven set to maintain a constant temperature of 65°C until no more water was lost as a result of drying. Using an electronic weighing scale, the weights of the seedlings—both fresh and dry—were recorded in grams. The quantum yield of photosystem II (ΦPSII) and the electron transport rate (ETR) in light-adapted leaves were measured with a pulse-amplitude modulation (PAM) fluorometer (LI-600 porometer/fluorometer, Lincoln, US) from 2nd upper-most fully expanded leaf of each plant and was calculated based on the quantum yield and the light intensity (Qamb). For measurement of photosynthesis related traits, the data from upper, middle and lower parts of 2nd topmost fully expanded leaves of each plant were collected (three times) and then averaged to get one value. Data from plants (n = 5) were gathered from 5 replicates having 9 plants each. The quantum yield of PSII (ΦPSII) was measured using the following formula:

where F’m is the maximum fluorescence yield in a light-adapted leaf and Fs is the steady-state fluorescence yield in a light-adapted leaf.

The electron transport rate (ETR) was calculated using the following formula:

where Qamb is the photosynthetically active radiation (PAR) in µmol.photons.m−2.s−1, 0.5 is the fraction of photosynthetic active radiation (PAR) absorbed by PSII, and 0.84 is the leaf absorptance.

For total soluble carbohydrates (TSC) in leaf tissues, the anthrone-H2SO4 method was employed using a spectrophotometer (UV spectrophotometer model UV-2600) set at 630 nm. The TSC were measured in 2nd upmost fully expanded leaves from NCQD treated and control plants, and TSC values were then compared to the absorbance of a glucose standard solution (200 µg/mL) to find percentages [72]. A portable chlorophyll meter (SPAD-502, Minolta, Japan) was used to measure total chlorophyl content in leaves [73].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Relative expression studies of key photosynthesis genes
ce:section.ce:para: The relative expression of key genes, involved in photosynthesis in leaf tissues from plants treated with NCQD versus the control group, was also examined. These genes included phosphoenolpyruvate carboxylase (ZmPEPC), ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate carboxylase/oxygenase (ZmRUB), pyruvate orthophosphate dikinase (ZmPPDK), NADP-malate dehydrogenase (ZmNADP-MDH), NADP-malic enzyme (ZmNADP-ME), and carbonic anhydrase (ZmCA). Using Trizol (Invitrogen), the total RNA was extracted from fresh leaf tissues in accordance with the manufacturer's instructions. To prevent gDNA and cDNA production, Prime ScriptTM reagent and a genomic DNA (gDNA) eraser kit (Takara, Co. Ltd., Japan) were used. Using the M-MLV Reverse Transcription Kit (Takara, Co. Ltd., Japan), the first strand of cDNA was produced. The produced cDNA was taken to qRT-PCR in a light cycler 96 machine (Roche, LightCycler® 96 well, 10498, Switzerland) using SYBR® Premix Ex TaqII (Takara, Co. Ltd., Japan). The reference genes, β-actin and β-tubulin, were selected based on their consistently constant expression levels across all samples. Using the NCBI (National Center for Biotechnology Information) sequencing database (http://www.ncbi. nlm.nih.gov/) and primer generation tools (SnapGene), the primers utilized in this experiment were produced. The target gene expression levels were determined by utilizing the 2-(∆∆Ct) technique to calculate their relative expression. The t-test was employed to evaluate the statistical significance of the variances.

ce:section.ce:section-title: NCQDs performance under different photosynthetic photon flux densities (PPFDs)
ce:section.ce:para: An investigation was carried out in a climate chamber designed for regulated plant growth with the aim of evaluating the effects of NCQDs on photosystem efficiency related measurements including TR, ФPSII, and ETR in connection with increasing PFD (photon flux density). In this experiment, 28-day-old plants were acclimated to different photon flux densities (PFDs) (100–400 μmole.m−2s−1) in an artificially controlled production chamber while maintaining equal root zone conditions and other parameters (temperature and relative humidity). For comparison, four plant treatments were optimized for 12 hours under four distinct PFDs (100, 200, 300, and 400 μmole. m−2 s−1) and the photosynthetic related data was collected after the plants (n = 6) were fully adapted to PFD. At the end of the day, ETR, TR, and ФPSII values were gathered from 2nd topmost fully expanded leaf of each plant from each group. For statistical analysis, these data were compared with those of the control group.

In order to ascertain the performance of bioderived NCQD as a light (<400 nm) harvester for its role in hybrid photosystems, the effects of UV radiation on maize leaves treated with NCQD (21 days after NCQD application) were evaluated [74]. In order to assess the photo-conversion capability of NCQDs as a hybrid photosynthesis system, the photosynthetic efficiency of plants treated with NCQDs and a control group without NCQDs was measured in a controlled environment using a ceiling box containing ultraviolet lamps (8 W) emitting 254 nm, 302 nm, and 365 nm kept at 40 cm distance from the plant canopy [75] for UV-C, UV-B and UV-A treatments, respectively in three separate settings. Three measurements per plant were performed from each group with the similar age and growth chosen, and data were obtained from the second upper-most fully expanded leaves of each plant (n = 6) after being exposed to UV radiations for the duration of 24 h. Data from each plant were collected as described in previous section. The experiment was repeated twice and the data values were averaged to get the final results in order to regulate the variables that might potentially impact the photosynthetic parameters. The other growth parameters including relative humidity (%) and temperature were kept normal. To measure their influence on the photosynthetic performance of the plants, the data regarding photosynthesis related traits including the chlorophyll a, b contents, the carotenoid levels, the maximum quantum efficiency (F v /F m ) and maximum florescence (F m ) in dark-adapted leaves, were collected immediately after the UV-C treatment of 24 hrs. Whereas the data for quantum yield of photosystem II (ФPSII) and the electron transport rate (ETR) were collected from light-adapted leaves after acclimatizing the treated plants for 12 hours under normal light (600 µmol m−2 s−1 intensity) in growth chamber.

The statistical software Statistix Suite 8.1 was utilized to conduct one-way ANOVA on the acquired data from replicates of each treatment in the studies. The information is displayed as mean values with standard deviation, and the Fisher's least significant difference (LSD) test was used to determine the statistical difference between treatments. Significance levels were set at p<0.01, p<0.001, and p<0.0001, denoted by single (*), double (**), and triple asterisks (***), respectively. The data was shown using the GraphPad Prism 8.0 program [76].

The spherical morphology of the as-prepared NCQDs clusters can be seen in TEM images ( Fig. 1A) and their nano-sizes <10 nm (Fig. 1B). The elemental analysis (EDX spectroscopy) revealed C:N:O proportions were 57.76:20.82:21.31 % (Fig. 1 C) in NCQDs which implies high nitrogen contents [77]. Under UV-stereomicroscope (Nikon, DS-Ri2, Tokyo, Japan), with excitation of UV lamp, the blue fluorescing CQD clusters are visible (Fig. 1D). The NCQDs showed an ivory-white hue under standard lighting (Fig. 1E); whereas, after exposed to UV light, the NCQDs solution showed a bright blue fluorescence (Fig. 1 F), which could be seen without any optical tools. In UV–vis absorption spectra, a peak in UV-region at 324 nm was observed in absorbance spectra of NCQD solution in DMSO (Fig. 1 G). Conversely, DMSO (blank) did not display any peaks. The optimal emission of NCQD photoluminescence (PL) spectrum spans the wavelength range of 400 nm to 580 nm. Notably, when the excitation wavelength was adjusted at 350 – 390 nm, the largest emission peak was observed between 400 – 500 nm (blue light range), with maximum peaks recorded around 445 nm. Excitation and emission ranges of NCQDs are given in graph (Fig. 1H) against corresponding excitation wavelengths ranging from 320 to 390 nm.

A broad XRD peak at 2θ angles of 19.1º (∼20º) and a relatively weak and broad peak of 34.3º [28] corresponds to the graphitic carbon (002) and (100) hkl plane (JCPDS card no. 26–1076), denoting to semi-graphitic amorphous carbon structures (C–C), as shown in Fig. 1I. Diffraction peaks for C–N are normally located in the 30°–40° 2θ range [28] and peaks observed at around 36° 2θ are for those related to C–O [78,79]. A larger wide peak near 20º (2θ) at the beginning of XRD indicates the NCQDs are mostly amorphous, indicating a graphitic carbon lacking perfect crystallinity [80]. NCQDs were electronegative with a net negative zeta potential of −7.21±0.35 mV (Fig. 1 J).

The XPS survey spectrum of as-synthesized NCQDs showed a prominent C1s peak at about 285.1 eV, a clear N1s peak at 398.4 eV and an obvious O1s peak at 531.8 eV ( Fig. 2 A). Furthermore, the N1s spectrum also showed two distinct peaks denoting to pyridinic– and graphitic–N associated to graphene lattice plane of CQDs, the O1s spectrum showed CO and C–OH peaks and the C1s spectrum showed C–N, C–C and C–O peaks. To further explore the functional groups of synthesized NCQDs, the FT-IR peaks were identified and interpreted as displayed in Fig. 2B. Stretching vibrations of the strong –OH and –NH functional groups, associated with alcohol and amines, respectively, are responsible for a broad peak observed around 3378 cm−1. Furthermore, stretching vibrations of the C–H bonds of sp3 hybridized carbon atoms (alkanes) are responsible for a sharp peak at 2929 cm−1. A sharp FT-IR peak at 1664 cm⁻¹ corresponds to the C–N bond corresponding to secondary amines (C=NH) and amides (OC–N). At wavenumbers of 1413 cm−1, the stretching of broad and moderate peaks of C–H bonds associated with alkyl groups was observed. The medium peaks at 1154 and 1080 cm⁻¹ region correspond to C–N bonds of aliphatic amines and C–O bonds, respectively; whereas, strong stretching peaks of N–H bonds were observed at 1024 cm−1 wavenumber of FTIR spectra. Furthermore, weak and sharp peaks in the fingerprint region associated with CC and C–H stretching vibrations of aromatic compounds were also observed.

NMR spectra (Fig. 2 C) of as-synthesized NCQDs, dissolved in DMSO-d6, indicate various multiplicities of proton environment associated with hydrogen (1H) and carbon (13C) in various functional moieties with multiple peak intensities. In 13C NMR spectra, peaks around 100 ppm suggest sp2 hybridized carbons, found in alkenes and aromatic hydrocarbon rings; whereas peaks between 70–80 ppm are indicative of carbon atoms bonded with amines and alkyl groups. The chemical shift near 60 ppm observed in 13C spectra corresponds to a double bonded carbon atom in an alkene (H–C–H) or alcohol group (–OH). In 1H NMR spectra, a peak at 0–1 ppm corresponds to aliphatic hydrogens (protons) on sp3 hybridized (single bonds) carbons. These hydrogens are part of alkyl chains (carbon atoms connected in a linear or branched chain) and are directly bonded to carbon atoms (C–H) that are not attached to any electronegative atoms or functional groups. Peak at 2 ppm corresponds to hydrogens on carbons (–CH3) adjacent to carbonyl groups (CO). A short and high peak between 3 and 4 ppm is due to hydrogens near electronegative atoms (e.g., oxygen, nitrogen) indicating amino (–NH2) and hydroxy (–OH) groups attached to carbon atoms. In 1H NMR spectra, a peak at 5 ppm typically corresponds to hydrogens in an aromatic environment (hexagonal carbon rings); whereas the residual solvent peak at 2.5 ppm is typically due to DMSO-d6 [81].

In order to assess the cytotoxicity of the as-synthesized NCQDs, various concentrations (0.02, 0.2, 2, 20, 200 and 2000 µg/mL) were tested on HeLa cell lines. The metabolic activity of the cells after the MTT assay was measured using a spectrophotometer adjusted at OD570. Cell viability over 80 % was deemed indicative of minimal cytotoxicity [82]. The MTT test findings indicated that the treatment of all the solutions of organic green synthesized NCQDs exhibited negligible toxicity towards HeLa cell lines after exposure of 48 hours [83–86]. Results of HeLa cell viability percentages are given in Fig. 2D.

Seed priming with NCQDs significantly improved the germination of the seed, growth and dry weights of the shoot and root of the maize plant ( Fig. 3A–H). The results show that when NCQDs were applied at different concentration dosages, the seed germination was improved in comparison to the control group, which received no NCQDs. The data in Fig. 3A demonstrated that, in comparison to the control treatment, seed germination was significantly increased by 64.5 %, 70.9 %, and 67.74 % when treated with priming concentrations of 0.02 mg/g, 0.04 mg/g, and 0.06 mg/g, respectively. In a similar vein, root length and root dry weights were significantly increased when applying seed priming with NCQDs. As can be seen in Fig. 3B, when priming concentrations of NCQD0.02 and NCQD0.04 were applied, respectively, the root length increased significantly by 44.7 % and 89.7 %, relative to the control treatment. Similarly, when priming concentrations of NCQD0.02 and NCQD0.04 were applied, the dry weight of the roots showed a significant increase of 103.3 % and 133.6 %, respectively, in comparison to the control treatment (Fig. 3C). The data displayed indicates that the application of priming doses at NCQD0.02 and NCQD0.04 also led to a noteworthy improvement in shoot dry weight (Fig. 3E) with improvements of 46.4 % and 61.9 %, and shoot length, with increases of 37.3 % and 45.2 % (Fig. 3D), respectively, in comparison to the control treatments. The data in Fig. 3F–H showed that seed priming with 0.02 mg/g and 0.04 mg/g of NCQDs significantly increased the amount of chlorophyll-a by 140 % and 195 %, the chlorophyll-b by 225 % and 274 %, and the carotenoids by 99 % and 139 % in maize shoots, respectively.

When NCQD0.02 (0.02 g/L) was applied to the maize roots of a hydroponic system, root-shoot biomass and growth were significantly improved ( Fig. 4A–F). After 7-, 14-, 21-, 28-, and 35-days following germination denoted as D0, D7, D14, D21, D28, the parameters—plant height, maximum root length, and root-shoot growth parameters, fresh and dry weights—were measured. The NCQD0.02 dose was applied on D0, D7, D14, and D21 in order to observe the long-term effects of NCQDs in the leaf tissues of maize plants. After that, the outcomes from the treated maize seedlings were contrasted with those of the control group, which was not given any NCQDs. Standard Hoagland's nutrition medium was used to deliver nutrients in both treatments. The application of NCQD0.02 solution to the roots had a significant beneficial effect on plant height as compared to root length, according to the data. In particular, compared to the control treatment, there was a rise in plant height of 39.24 %, 33.62 %, 36.70 %, and 38.67 % after D7, D14, D21, and D28 respectively (Fig. 4B); and an increase in root length of 18.75 %, 20.25 %, 14.42, and 11.87 % after D7, D14, D21, and D28 (Fig. 4A). Similarly, when compared to the control treatment, the use of NCQD0.02 resulted in a significant increase in the fresh weight of newly grown root and shoots after D7, D14, D21, and D28, with increments of 116.90 %, 45.50 %, 38.51 %, 61.54 % (Figs. 4C) and 78.57 %, 39.74 %, 57.15 %, 76.62 % (Fig. 4D) respectively. When compared to the control treatment, the application of NCQD0.02 showed a significant improvement in dry root and shoots after D7, D14, D21, and D28, with increments of 38.56 %, 46.11 %, 36.74 %, 42.45 % (Figs. 4E) and 81.15 %, 59.93 %, 81.87 %, 87.97 % (Fig. 4F), respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: NCQDs enhanced carbohydrate synthesis and photosynthesis attributes
ce:section.ce:para: The supplementary effects of NCQDs on photosynthetic parameters, electron transport rate, and photosystem II quantum efficiency in maize plants were assessed, which, consequently, improved the synthesis of non-structural carbohydrate (starch), which is the main product of photosynthesis. After D28, or 28 days following NCQD treatment, the data were gathered. The results showed that, when compared to the control group, treatment NCQD0.02 had a significant (p<0.001) increase in the percentages of photosynthetic efficiency (69.7 %), stomatal conductance (73 %), and transpiration rate (67.2 %). As compared to the control treatment group ( Fig. 5A – H), the data showed that the ФPSII value of the NCQD0.02 treatment was 29.13 % higher; CO2 intercellular concentration by 69.5 %, electron transport rate by 63.3 %, intercellular to ambient CO2 conc. by 37.7 %, and total soluble carbohydrates by 32.8 %. Likewise, C a , C b , C c+x contents were increased by 96.64 %, 109.36 %, 162.7 % in NCQD0.02 treated plants as compared to control treatments; whereas TCC values were increased by 89.06 % (Fig. 5I – L).

ce:section.ce:section-title: NCQDs improved light harvesting capacity of plant
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the effects of NCQD0.02 on ETR, ФPSII, and TR in connection with the increase in light flux, 28 days old plants that showed consistent growth were exposed to different photon flux densities (PFDs), which ranged from 100 to 400 μmole.m−2s−1. Green synthesized organic NCQD improved photosynthetic efficiency by increasing ETR, TR, and quantum yield of photosystem II (ФPSII) under varying light intensities ranging from 100 to 400 μmole.m−2s−1. The results showed that the application of NCQD0.02 significantly (p<0.01) increased the ETR, TR, and ФPSII by 57.24.73 %, 51.63 and 15.13 % on average with incremental PFDs from 100 to 400 μmole.m−2s−1 ( Fig. 6A–C). Results further revealed that under 200 μmole.m−2s−1 light intensity, minimum increase in values for ETR (47.66 %), ФPSII (12.26 %) and TR (41.15 %) values were recorded for NCQD treated plants as compared to control plants; whereas, under 400 μmole.m−2s−1 light intensity, maximum increase in values for ETR (65.40 %), ФPSII (18.97 %) and TR (47.56 %) values were recorded for NCQD treated plants. Fig. 6D represents the visual depiction of the root and shoot of control and NCQD0.02 treated maize plants 28 days after treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: NCQDs enhanced relative expression of key photosynthesis genes
ce:section.ce:para: The application of NCQD0.02 solution to the maize roots in hydroponics resulted in a significant (p<0.01) increase in the relative expression of six genes i.e. ZmPEPC, ZmRUB, ZmPPDK, ZmNADP-MDH, ZmNADP-ME and ZmCA, related to carbon assimilation and CO2 fixation during photosynthesis in the leaf tissues, comparing to the control group (Fig. 6F–K). All genes were found to be highly responsive to the stimulating action of NCQDs. Upon treatment with NCQDs at a dose of 20 mg/L, the expression levels of six studied genes ZmPEPC, ZmRUB, ZmPPDK, ZmNADP-MDH, ZmNADP-ME and ZmCA were dramatically elevated by 24-fold, 19-fold, 16-fold, 14-fold, 63-fold and 10-fold respectively, after 28 days post-treatment (D28). These results aligned with the chlorophyll concentration and photosynthetic rate. The details of genes, primer sequences and their functions in photosynthesis were listed in supplementary tables S1 and S2.

The NCQDs in plant tissues were visually observed using a UV-stereomicroscope (Nikon, DS-Ri2, Tokyo, Japan) when they were subjected to UV light (λ = 330–380 nm). In treated maize leaves, a uniform dispersion of florescent NCQDs could be seen inside mesophyll cells in the upper and lower side of leaves, guard cells of stomata, and intercellular spaces ( Fig. 7A–F). Under the same conditions, the florescence was scarcely noticeable in the control group. Furthermore, it was evident from the fluorescence and the contour of different tissues that NCQDs could readily translocate from roots to leaves and penetrate diverse tissue cells. Therefore, it is reasonable to believe that NCQDs are taken up by plant roots and subsequently moved from the roots to the stem and leaves.

Data presents on photosynthesis-related features ( Fig. 8A–G), comparing them with the control group for the purpose of evaluating the performance of NCQDs as a hybrid photosynthesis system under high UV-C (λ=254 nm), UV-B (λ=302 nm) and UV-A (λ=365 nm) treatment. The results demonstrated that the NCQD treatment had a substantial positive impact on electron transport rate (ETR), increasing it by 168 %. Similarly, maximum fluorescence (Fm), photosynthesis efficiency (Fv/Fm) and quantum yield of photosystem II (ФPSII) were also considerably enhanced by 22 %, 237 % and 187 % respectively. The chlorophyll a (C a ) content in NCQD treated plants increased by 75 %, while the chlorophyll b (C b ) content increased by 19.6 % and the carotenoid content (C x+c ) increased by 98.6 % compared to the control group after 18 hours of UV-C treatment. Likewise, under UV-B treatment, NCQD treatment significantly improved ETR, Fm, Fv/Fm, ФPSII by 72.23 %, 54.75 %, 57.59 %, 245 % as compared to control; and C a , C b , C x+c contents were increased by 77.53 %, 65.35 % and 124.69 % respectively. In case of UV-A treatment, and C a , C b , C x+c contents were enhanced by 24,5 %, 49.05 % and 34.43 % respectively, in treated groups; whereas ETR, Fm, Fv/Fm, ФPSII by 67.68 %, 43.04 %, 25.16 %, 21.28 % respectively, as compared to the control groups.

Different studies have synthesized N-doped CDs attached with NH2, COOH and OH functional groups, utilizing natural organic compounds containing N and C sources [87–91]. However, attainable N content in N-doped CDs was very limited due to the scarcity of free N-associated functional groups to be incorporated into CDs [92]. In this sustainable NCQD synthesis, HCl was used to facilitate the hydrolysis of starch and protein molecules present in wheat flour, whereas microwave radiation was employed to the formation of NCQDs from the fragmented molecules [93]. After heat-assisted acid hydrolysis, the fragmented carbohydrate molecules containing carbon undergo a process of rearrangement during microwave heating, leading to the formation of amorphous carbon core (C-C) of NCQDs. Functional groups such as primary amines (NH2), secondary amines (NH), carboxyl (COOH), alcohol (OH), alkyl (–CH, =CH2, CH3) and arene (C6H6), were additionally incorporated onto the NCQD surface during micro-wave treatment [94–96] which was also confirmed by FT-IR and NMR spectra of as-prepared NCQDs (Fig. 2 A and 2B). Fig. 9 demonstrates a pictorial illustration of the proposed chemical synthesis of fluorescent NCQDs. Green synthesized NCQDs exhibited a spherical morphology with a higher abundance of oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups. Due to the higher proportion of N associated functionalities provided by amino acids, a higher proportion of N content (20.82 %) was observed in synthesized NCQDs compared to previously studied N-doped CQDs (Fig. 2 C) [97]. Additionally, their characteristic bright blue fluorescence (λmax. ∼ 445 nm) under UV light (Fig. 2H) is attributed to their smaller size and non-conjugated structure [98].

The study utilized wheat flour derived N-functionalized CQDs, synthesized using a green approach, with the objective to enhance the growth and biomass in maize. Our study (in vivo) demonstrated that surface-passivated bio-derived NCQDs can effectively convert photosynthetically inactive range (λ<400 nm) of light into PAR range, which could be utilized by photosynthetic machinery as a hybrid photosynthesis system, in conjunction with chloroplasts within plant leaves, boosting starch production. Previous studies have shown that the quantum yields (QYs) of CQDs can also be enhanced up to 55–60 % by surface passivation with N [99]. The UV absorbance of NCQDs and increased biocatalytic activity are also due to highly dense negative charges on the surface of NCQDs [100,101]. The absorbance (Abs.) and emittance (PL) spectra of NCQDs demonstrate their capacity to absorb the ultraviolet (UV) portion of sunlight and convert it into the photosynthetically active radiation (PAR) range (Fig. 2 G and 2 H). This PAR range can be utilized by chloroplasts to enhance the efficiency of photosynthesis in plants, enabling the conversion of light energy into chemical energy. Consequently, this process led to an increase in starch production and biomass increase (Fig. 5). For investigation on plant photosynthesis, the entry of NCQDs into plant cells was facilitated by their negative ζ-potential [28]. Previous studies have demonstrated that electronegative CQDs can effectively serve as electron donors and acceptors in energy conversion and electron transfer processes [28,29,102]. Therefore, electronegative surface charge also contributed to increased electron transport rate [103]. The net negative charge (-7.21 mV) on the surface (Fig. 1 J) is attributed to the presence of free electrons provided by functional groups such as amino, carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups [104–108].

Whole grain wheat flour contains a higher proportion of nitrogen rich amino acids, including glutamic acid, proline, leucine, aspartic acid, phenylalanine, arginine and traces of other amino acids [109–111]. The functional groups associated with these amino acids are displayed in Fig. 9. The synthesized NCQDs mostly obtained functional groups such as amines and amides from protein molecules, whereas alkyl groups were also derived from starch molecules. Previous studies showed that aliphatic amines (C-H) are typically delivered by nitrogen-containing precursors like amino acids during the process of CD synthesis [112]. Similarly, starch molecules in wheat flour are mostly amylose and amylopectin, which largely contain sp³ hybridized carbon atoms [113]. Whereas, central carbon atom in different amino acids is sp² hybridized, attached with four functional groups, including amino group (−NH₂), carboxyl group (−COOH), a hydrogen atom (−H), and a variable side chain (−R group) [114]. The prominent and broad XRD peak (Fig. 1I) observed around 19 – 20° (2θ) indicates a nanosized material having disordered carbon structures, specifically attributed to sp3 carbon atoms, generally found in starch polymers [115]. Likewise, the presence of different carbon associated functionalities may cause a broad X-ray diffraction peak at around 34.3° (2θ), indicating a surface N functionalizing impact of NCQDs. The presence of broad peaks at these angles suggests turbostratic carbon phase of NCQDs having imperfectly aligned semi-graphitic structures [116]. In a prior study, Bajpai et al. [117] investigated blue-emitting CQDs generated from a milk protein employing hydrothermal treatment and found a broad X-ray diffraction peak around 20° (2θ) indicating amorphous carbon phase (002) of CQDs. Teng et al. [118] observed two broad peaks near 21° and 42° (2θ) in X-ray diffraction pattern corresponding to carbon phases (002) and (100) owing to N-doping effect. Arul et al. [119] also observed an intense broad and a weak peak of 28.5° and 40.6° (2θ) in XRD of N-doped CQDs, which were allocated to the C(002) and C(100) planes of graphite, respectively. Therefore, it can be inferred that the synthesized NCQDs possess an amorphous structure inside and the graphitic structure linked to functional groups externally. The XPS spectra also confirmed that the NCQD functional groups include sp2 hybridized carbon structures derived from hydrolyzed protein frameworks associated with diverse N and O functional groups [118]. Previous studies showed that the cytotoxicity of CQDs could be significantly reduced by covering them with biocompatible polymers [120]. Another study showed that adding peptides and amino acids to the surface of CQDs may improve their biocompatibility and also reduce their cytotoxicity towards different cell lines [121]. Biopolymer and protein-based encapsulation has been used as a substitute tactic to alleviate the cytotoxic consequences of CQD-based nanocomposites [122]. Therefore, the envelope of amino acids, with its numerous functional groups, could also be the reason for the non-toxic nature of synthesized amorphous NCQDs (Fig. 2C), which can be explored further [82,123].

The hydroponic studies (Fig. 4) further demonstrated that applying NCQD0.02 to the roots increased average plant height in treated plants, increased the average root and shoot fresh weights, dry weights, and showed significant effects on chlorophyll contents. The study results further revealed that NCQDs can be transferred from the roots to different leaf tissues, where they participated in the process of light harvesting, electron transport chain and Calvin cycle ( Fig. 10). The Calvin cycle is a central process in photosynthesis that converts CO2 into starch (process known as CO2 fixation), utilizing adenosine triphosphate (ATP) and nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate (NADPH) generated as a result of light reactions involving electron transport chain ( Fig. 11). This phenomenon can be related to the quantum confinement effect and electron donation by NCQDs, which also resulted in intensified carboxylation process; hence, augmenting production of carbohydrates (starch) and led to a rapid increase in biomass. The potential interprets underlying the action of promoting photosynthesis may also be associated with the increased synthesis of enzymes relevant to photosynthesis, including RuBisCO, PEPC, PPDK, NADP-ME, NADP-MDH and CA, due to upregulation of key photosynthesis genes associated with these enzymes (Supplementary table S2) (Fig. 6). These enzymes play crucial roles in starch production (product of Calvin cycle) and various other related metabolic pathways involved in CO₂ fixation, carboxylation and carbon assimilation in C4 photosynthesis [124]. Therefore, enhancing the activity the enzymes or expression of their associated genes could further optimize C4 photosynthesis in maize plants, improving photosynthesis efficiency, starch synthesis, and consequently biomass (Fig. 11).

Photosynthesis efficiency was also increased due to NCQD application under all three UV treatments (UV-A, B and C); enhancing plant health status by UV conversion into PAR and sustaining the photosynthesis machinery under UV light exposures. Whereas control plants were strongly impacted by UV treatments with the order of effects being UV-C>UV-B>UV-A, depicting UV-C affected the plant photosynthesis mechanism the most (Fig. 8). NCQD treated plants showed comparatively better performance under UV-A treatments as compared to UV-B and UV-C treatment due to the harvesting ability of NCQD falling under UV-A and UV-B wavelength range. The improvement may also be ascribed to the UV harvesting and photo-excited electron donor properties of NCQDs, which possibly enhanced photosynthesis under UV exposure [125]. Overall, our experimental findings offer evidence that the photon harvesting ability of non-toxic NCQDs can enhance the efficiency of chloroplasts, resulting in a hybrid light harvesting system. This system effectively absorbs UV light, promotes electron transfer, improves photosystem efficiency, upregulates genes related to photosynthesis, maintains chlorophyll levels, and ultimately facilitates biomass production in maize plants.

In this work, we synthesized N-functionalized CQDs with abundant nitrogen-based functional groups by a simple, low-cost, and green method utilizing whole grain wheat flour as a precursor. We achieved this by exposing long chain polymers in wheat flour, rich in N and C, to acid-induced hydrolysis, followed by microwave treatment. The absorbance, excitation, and emission spectra showed that NCQDs can absorb UV light ranging from 320 – 390 nm and can emit blue florescence, while the maximum florescence was observed ∼445 nm. Due to their negative ζ-potential, capacity to absorb UV light and emit fluorescence in the blue spectrum, the bioderived NCQDs imitated the chlorophyll pigments (a and b), which resulted in substantial enhancement in various aspects of photosynthesis, electron transport chain, plant growth, and biomass production. Additionally, it also showed that these could upregulate genes, associated with photosynthesis cycle enzymes, regulating various aspects of the carbon fixation cycle inside leaf tissues. Furthermore, cytotoxicity tests of as-synthesized NCQDs demonstrated negligible toxicity towards HeLa cells, even at high concentrations of 2000 µg/mL. This study introduces the novel concept of utilizing bioderived fluorescent NCQDs as a promising sustainable option for enhancing photosynthesis and growth in plants.
